worth_it_explanation: A commercial break in Connected TV (CTV) is built from a pod, and the pod's settings decide how many ads play back to back, how long each one runs, and which advertisers can sit beside each other. Defaults left untouched can produce breaks that are too long, too repetitive, or stacked with rival brands, all of which push viewers to abandon the stream and pull down the price the inventory clears at. The check is a review of pod settings the publisher already configured in Equativ, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete pod decision the publisher can adjust.
intro: An ad pod is the set of ads that fill one commercial break in CTV and Over-the-Top (OTT) video. Its configuration decides the total break length, how many ads play back to back, the duration of each slot, and the rules that keep competing or repeating advertisers apart. This subcategory looks at whether a publisher's pod settings on Equativ are deliberate choices that fit the content and the viewer rather than platform defaults carried forward. These checks confirm the pod structure protects the viewing experience and the value of the break instead of trading both away to a setting nobody revisited.

audit_checks.title: Pod structure runs on defaults rather than deliberate settings
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Pod duration, the cap on ads per pod, and slot durations set how long a break runs and how it is divided. Defaults carried forward can produce breaks that are too long or too fragmented for the content, which raises abandonment and lowers the price the break clears at, so a pod that was never configured to fit the inventory leaves both experience and revenue exposed.

educate.base:
An ad pod is the container for a single commercial break in Connected TV (CTV) video, and its shape comes from a few settings the publisher controls on Equativ. Pod duration sets the total length of the break. A cap on the number of ads sets how many spots can play back to back inside that length. Slot duration, the minimum and maximum length of each ad, decides whether the break fills with a few long spots, many short ones, or a mix. These settings travel into the bid request as the pod opportunity, which is the structure the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab standardized in OpenRTB 2.6 so a buyer can fill a break in a way that fits the duration on offer.

The settings matter because a break that fits the content holds the viewer, and a break that does not pushes them to leave. A long pod stacked with many short ads reads as an interruption that overstays its welcome. A pod whose slot durations do not match the creative the demand actually carries leaves slots unfilled or forces a mismatch. The point of configuring the pod is to make the break a deliberate fit for the program and the audience rather than a length and shape that arrived by default.

Defaults are the risk here. A pod left at whatever Equativ or the SSAI setup applied out of the box is a structure nobody chose for this inventory. It may run longer than the content can carry, divide into more slots than the demand can fill at a good price, or set slot durations that do not match the typical creative length in the break. None of that shows up as an error. It shows up as a break that clears low and an audience that drops off.

A few signs point to pod settings that were never deliberately set:
  • Pod duration and the per-pod ad cap match the platform default rather than a length chosen for the content type
  • Slot duration ranges do not reflect the creative lengths the break's demand typically carries, for example 15-second and 30-second spots
  • No record exists of why the current pod length and structure were chosen, only that they have not changed
You may want to confirm that each pod's duration, ad cap, and slot durations were set on purpose for the content they run against. One approach is to read the break length and fill rate by content type, then size each pod to the program rather than to a single default.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming media property, a short clip and a long-form episode carry very different tolerance for break length, so a single default pod applied to both will overstay on the short content or underfill on the long. You may want to set pod duration and the ad cap by content length, keeping breaks in short content tight and reserving longer pods for the long-form inventory that can carry them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-driven streaming property, a shoppable or product-adjacent placement reaches an audience closer to a purchase and can justify a deliberately shorter, higher-value break rather than a long default pod. You may want to size those pods to protect the experience around the commercial content, so a high-intent moment is not buried under a long string of back-to-back ads.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server Video Endpoint for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review each pod's duration, its cap on ads per pod, and its slot duration range, then check those settings against the content the pod runs against and the demand it carries.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the CTV or video ad serving configuration and list the active ad pods or break definitions.
For each pod, record the pod duration, the maximum number of ads per pod, and the minimum and maximum slot durations.
Note which content types or placements each pod applies to, and whether the same default pod is reused across content of very different lengths.
Ask the publisher why each pod's length and structure were chosen, and record whether a deliberate reason exists or the settings are unchanged defaults.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by pod or content type, with break length, ads served per break, fill rate, and average price per break.
Compare the break length and fill rate across content types to see whether one pod length is being applied to content that should carry different breaks.
Note any pod where slots go unfilled or the break clears low, which points to a structure that does not fit the inventory.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per pod, the pod duration, the per-pod ad cap, and the slot duration range, alongside the content type it serves and a stated reason for the settings. A pod whose duration, cap, and slot durations were set deliberately for the content it runs against passes. A pod left at platform defaults, reused across content of very different lengths with no stated reason and with unfilled slots or low break prices, is a fail recorded with the pods and content types affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each pod's duration, ad cap, and slot durations alongside the content it serves above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods on platform defaults rather than settings chosen for the content each break serves. Pod duration, the cap on ads per pod, and the slot durations decide how long a break runs and how it is divided, and a default carried across content of different lengths can produce breaks that overstay on short content or underfill on long content, which raises abandonment and lowers the price the break clears at. {{voice_recommend}} reading break length and fill rate by content type in Equativ, then sizing each pod's duration, ad cap, and slot durations to the program it runs against rather than to a single default. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price per break to confirm the pod fits the inventory.

audit_checks.title: Competitive separation and frequency within pods are not configured
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without competitive separation, two ads from rival advertisers or the same product category can land in one break, and without a frequency rule the same ad can repeat across slots. Both read as a broken viewing experience that drives abandonment, and the clash can violate the exclusivity a direct-sold buyer was promised, so an unconfigured pod risks both the viewer and the advertiser relationship.

educate.base:
Once a pod can hold several ads back to back, two questions decide whether the break feels coherent or broken. The first is competitive separation, which keeps ads from rival advertisers or the same product category out of the same break. The second is frequency within the pod, which keeps a single ad from repeating across the slots of one break. Both are rules the publisher sets, and both protect the viewer from a break that reads as a mistake.

Competitive separation, also called exclusivity, is the practice of preventing ads from the same industry group from appearing in the same pod or next to each other inside it. Prebid documents this for long-form video through brand category translation, where each bid carries a category and the server enforces the separation rule across the pod. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab Content Taxonomy supplies the category framework that makes this possible. When separation is off, a viewer can watch two competing car brands, or two ads for the same product, play one after the other in a single break, which is the kind of clash that breaks immersion and undercuts both advertisers.

Frequency within the pod is the companion rule. A pod with several slots and no per-break frequency cap can serve the same creative twice in the same break if the demand allows it. That repetition is more grating in a CTV break than in a web page because the viewer is captive to the stream and cannot scroll past it. A per-pod or per-break frequency rule keeps one advertiser, or one creative, from filling slots it should not.

There is a contractual edge to this as well. A direct-sold buyer paying for a placement may have been promised category exclusivity in the break. If competitive separation is not enforced in the pod logic, a programmatic ad can land beside them and break that promise, which is a problem for the advertiser relationship and not only the viewer.

A few signs point to separation and frequency rules that are not configured:
  • The pod or break logic has no competitive separation or exclusivity rule enabled, so category clashes are possible
  • No brand category or industry mapping feeds the separation logic, which means the platform has nothing to separate on
  • No per-pod or per-break frequency cap exists, so the same advertiser or creative can repeat within one break
You may want to confirm that competitive separation and a per-break frequency rule are enabled and that a category mapping feeds the separation logic. One approach is to review the exclusivity settings on each pod alongside the category data the demand carries, then watch a sample of filled breaks to confirm no clashes or repeats slip through.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming media property carrying both direct-sold and programmatic demand in the same break, an exclusivity promise made to a direct buyer can be broken by a programmatic ad in an adjacent slot. You may want to confirm the pod's competitive separation respects any category exclusivity sold directly, so the break honors the direct contract rather than placing a rival beside it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-driven streaming property, a break adjacent to product or shoppable content can pull demand from competing retailers or product categories, where a clash is both jarring and commercially counterproductive. You may want to set competitive separation by product category and a per-break frequency cap so a single break does not stack rival products or repeat one advertiser to a captive, purchase-minded audience.

learn_more.source_title: Show OTT Video Ads with Prebid for Header Bidding
Setting up Prebid Video in FreeWheel

investigate.intro: Review whether competitive separation and a per-break frequency rule are enabled on each pod, and confirm that a category mapping feeds the separation logic.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open each ad pod or break definition in the CTV or video configuration and locate its competitive separation, exclusivity, or category-conflict settings.
Record whether competitive separation is enabled and whether a brand category or industry mapping is in place for the separation logic to act on.
Locate the frequency settings for the pod and record whether a per-pod or per-break frequency cap limits how often one advertiser or creative can appear in a single break.
Note any direct-sold placement in these breaks that carries a category-exclusivity promise, and check whether the pod logic enforces it against programmatic demand.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting and break review
steps: Pull a sample of filled breaks, by recorded session or by report, that show the advertisers and categories served in each pod.
Review the sample for breaks where rival advertisers or the same product category appeared together, or where one creative repeated within a break.
Note the pods and content types where clashes or repeats occurred, which points to separation or frequency rules that are off or have no category data to act on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per pod, whether competitive separation is enabled, whether a category mapping feeds it, and whether a per-break frequency cap exists, alongside a sample review of filled breaks. A pod with separation enabled, a category mapping in place, and a frequency cap, with no clashes or repeats in the sample, passes. A pod with separation off, no category mapping, or no frequency cap, or one where the break sample shows category clashes or repeated creatives, is a fail recorded with the pods affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether competitive separation, a category mapping, and a per-break frequency cap are in place for each pod above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not enforce competitive separation and a per-break frequency rule within its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods. Without separation, rival advertisers or the same product category can play back to back in one break, and without a frequency cap the same creative can repeat across the slots, both of which read as a broken viewing experience and can violate a category-exclusivity promise made to a direct-sold buyer. {{voice_recommend}} enabling competitive separation on each pod, confirming a brand category mapping feeds it, and setting a per-break frequency cap, then reviewing a sample of filled breaks to confirm no clashes or repeats slip through. After the change, keep the separation and frequency rules in place across the pods that carry mixed demand.
